frontmatter.title: The Phoa axiom and synthetic quasicoherence

mainmatter.p: Can the Phoa axiom in synthetic domain theory be deduced from synthetic quasicoherence? I believe that Paul Taylor may have commented on something in this direction once in MathOverflow, but I do not know if anyone has proved this. I think it could be an interesting test-case. In cases where we have a clean understanding of the geometric theory in question (as in simplicial sets), this might be feasible; but I wonder about more sophisticated models of synthetic domain theory.

The map \Sigma ^ \Sigma \to \Sigma \times \Sigma sending f to the pair { \mathopen {} \left ( f \bot ,f \top \right ) \mathclose {}} is an embedding and its image is the subset of \Sigma \times \Sigma spanned by pairs { \mathopen {} \left ( p,q \right ) \mathclose {}} such that p= \top implies q= \top.

Let \mathbb {T} be a horn theory. The generic \mathbb {T}-model U_{ \mathbb {T} } is quasicoherent in the following sense: internal to \mathbf {Set} { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathbb {T} \right ] \mathclose {}}, for any finitely presentable U_{ \mathbb {T} }-algebra A, the canonical U_{ \mathbb {T} }-algebra homomorphism A \to U_{ \mathbb {T} } ^{ \operatorname {Spec} {A}} sending a to \lambda f: \operatorname {Spec} {A}. f \, a is an isomorphism, where \operatorname {Spec} {A} is the synthetic spectrum of A.
